subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: governance/rights/politics built from independent sources (IMF, World Bank, AfDB, V-Dem, Freedom House, RSF, HRW, Anti-Slavery International, US TIP Report, BTI, ICG) + international wire; Mauritanian state media EXCLUDED there, economics IMF-validated.

Comparatively stable for the region: President Ghazouani won a 2024 second (and final) term (~56%) and the 2019 handover was Mauritania's first peaceful elected-to-elected transition - but power remains military-rooted and competition constrained.

Standout flags: a security success - no jihadist attack since ~2011 via a non-aggression posture, hardened intelligence, and the Mehari border guard, even as JNIM presses Mali's nearby Kayes region; a persistent chattel-slavery legacy (~149,000 still enslaved, descent-based, Haratine victims) with reformist courts but ongoing arrests of anti-slavery (IRA) activists; and new offshore GTA gas (BP/Kosmos, first LNG export 2025) layered on iron ore and rich-but-overfished waters.

Currency: the ouguiya (MRU) is a sovereign, lightly-managed float (since the Dec 2023 interbank FX market, a +/-5% band vs a USD-heavy basket) - judged on the Central Bank of Mauritania's own credibility; not a reserve currency.

economic.notes: An IMF-praised, prudently-managed extractive economy (iron ore, fishing, now offshore gas) with low inflation and falling debt, against resource concentration and overfishing risk.

fiscal_state.notes: Markedly improved - deficit near ~0.3% of GDP in 2025 on higher extractive revenue, public debt declining (~36% external), moderate distress risk, IMF-program backed. (IMF; AfDB; World Bank)

monetary_independence.notes: A sovereign, lightly-managed float (interbank FX market since Dec 2023); the central bank has gained some credibility but limited depth and capacity. (IMF; State Dept ICS)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The ouguiya is not an international reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Low and well-managed - around ~1.5-2.5% in 2024-25, with a modest rise expected on global food/energy pass-through. (IMF; AfDB)

banking_stability.notes: Small, concentrated banking sector with limited penetration but no acute distress signal. (IMF; State Dept ICS)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Improving trajectory as GTA gas (first LNG export 2025) joins iron ore (~50% of exports) and fishing (~10% of GDP), but a resource-concentrated profile with serious overfishing/stock-depletion risk. (World Bank; AfDB; IMF)

institutional.notes: Military-rooted governance with weak-but-engaging institutions, constrained but not crushed press, and a justice system uneven on slavery enforcement.

rule_of_law.notes: Limited judicial independence and entrenched elite/military influence, though it has built specialized anti-slavery/trafficking courts (a 2025 Special Tribunal). (Freedom House; V-Dem; US TIP; BTI)

statistical_integrity.notes: Usable and actively IMF-engaged on macro data, with capacity gaps. (IMF)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Moderate-to-weak capacity, reform-oriented under the IMF program but thin human capital (HCI ~0.38). (World Bank; IMF)

press_freedom.notes: Partly free relative to the region but constrained, with arrests of activist-journalists (e.g. IRA-linked) in 2025. (RSF; HRW; UNPO)

political_social.notes: Relatively stable elected-military governance, undercut by a persistent slavery legacy, ethnic/caste hierarchy, and constrained activism.

political_stability.notes: Comparatively stable - Ghazouani's 2024 re-election followed Mauritania's first peaceful elected-to-elected handover in 2019, though power stays military-rooted. (Freedom House; BTI; Reuters)

civil_liberties.notes: Partly Free with real constraints - 2025 arrests of anti-slavery (IRA) activists and journalists, and discrimination against Haratine/Afro-Mauritanians. (Freedom House; HRW; UNPO; Amnesty)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: A developing legal framework praised by a UN rapporteur, but reports of irregular-migrant deportations and detention along the EU-funded migration route. (OHCHR; HRW; State Dept ICS)

social_cohesion.notes: A deep, unresolved fault line: descent-based chattel slavery persists (~149,000 enslaved) atop a White-Moor / Haratine / Afro-Mauritanian hierarchy. (Anti-Slavery Intl; US TIP; HRW)

demographics.notes: Young and growing, but weak human capital (high stunting, low schooling) limits the dividend. (World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: A reliable, Western-partnered counterterrorism actor that has avoided attacks for over a decade, exposed to a worsening Sahel/JNIM threat on its eastern border.

alliance_reliability.notes: A steady EU/US/Gulf security and migration partner (EU-funded Mehari guard), more consistent than its junta-run neighbors. (ICG; EuroMeSCo; State Dept ICS)

conflict_involvement.notes: No jihadist attack since ~2011, but JNIM's surge in Mali's adjacent Kayes region presses the remote eastern border (the Route de l'Espoir corridor). (ICG; Africa Center; Soufan Center; ACLED)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Not sanctioned; a managed float with some FX frictions but no severe capital-control regime. (IMF; State Dept ICS)

physical_practical.notes: Harsh desert climate, weak healthcare and human capital, thin infrastructure, with relatively low violent-crime exposure.

climate.notes: Saharan/Sahel drought, desertification, sandstorms, and low-lying Nouakchott coastal/sea-level risk. (ND-GAIN; WB)

healthcare.notes: Weak system with poor outcomes and thin coverage outside Nouakchott (HCI ~0.38, high stunting). (WHO; World Bank)

infrastructure.notes: Sparse over vast desert, though extractive/port and new gas infrastructure are upgrading along the coast. (World Bank; AfDB)

crime_safety.notes: Relatively low violent crime by regional standards; main risks are remote-area banditry and Sahel-spillover exposure. (OSAC; State Dept ICS)

disaster_insurance.notes: Negligible insurance penetration against drought/heat exposure. (inference)

summary: By regional standards Mauritania is comparatively stable and well-managed: President Ghazouani won a 2024 second term after the country's first peaceful elected-to-elected handover in 2019, the IMF praises prudent fiscal and monetary policy (deficit near balance, falling debt, ~1.5-2.5% inflation), and new offshore GTA gas (first LNG export 2025) adds to iron ore and rich fishing - a positive economic skew. It is also a rare Sahel security success, with no jihadist attack since around 2011 and a reliable Western counterterrorism partnership, though JNIM's advance into Mali's neighboring Kayes region now pressures its remote eastern border. The dominant drags are social and institutional rather than economic: a persistent descent-based chattel-slavery legacy (~149,000 still enslaved) atop a rigid ethnic-caste hierarchy, ongoing arrests of anti-slavery activists, military-rooted governance with limited rule of law, weak human capital, and a harsh desert climate. Net negative overall, but materially less so - and with a better trajectory - than its junta-run, conflict-torn neighbors.
